questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.question_text:
The table below shows some information of three atoms :
Number of protonsNumber of electronsNumber of neutrons
Protium110
Deuterium111
Oxygen888

sub_questions.label: a
sub_questions.question_text: Explain why protium and deuterium are isotopes.
sub_questions.answer:
Protium and deuterium have same number of protons but different numbers of neutrons. / 
Protium and deuterium have same atomic number but different mass numbers.

sub_questions.label: b
sub_questions.question_text:
Deuterium can be represented by D. It reacts with oxygen as shown in the equation below :

\(2D_2 + O_2 \rightarrow 2D_2O\)

Draw the electron diagram for a D₂O molecule, showing ELECTRONS IN THE OUTERMOST SHELLS only.
sub_questions.answer: [IMG:ans_q1_a_0.jpg]

sub_questions.label: c
sub_questions.question_text: A small piece of sodium metal is placed into liquid D₂O at room conditions.

children.label: i
children.question_text: State TWO expected observations.
children.answer:
Any TWO of the following (1 mark for each point) :
• Colourless gas evolves.
• Sodium metal dissolves. / Sodium drags / moves on the surface of  \( D_{2}O(l) \) .
• Sparks are observed. / Flame is observed. / Sodium burns. / Heat evolves. / White fume evolves. / Hissing sound is heard.

children.label: ii
children.question_text: Write the chemical equation for the reaction involved.
children.answer: \( 2Na + 2D_{2}O \rightarrow 2NaOD + D_{2} \)

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.question_text: Sodium chloride crystal has a giant ionic structure.

sub_questions.label: a
sub_questions.question_text:
The diagram below shows a part of the structure of sodium chloride crystal with some ions missing.

[IMG:q2_img_0.png]
sub_questions.answer: [IMG:ans_q2_1.jpg]

sub_questions.label: b
sub_questions.question_text: From an experiment, it was found that there are  \( 4 \, Na^{+} \)  ions and  \( 4 \, Cl^{-} \)  ions in a cube of sodium chloride crystal of volume  \( 1.80 \times 10^{-22} \, cm^{3} \) .

children.label: i
children.question_text:
Express the total mass of  \( 4 \, Na^{+} \)  ions and  \( 4 \, Cl^{-}\) ions in terms of the Avogadro's constant L.
(Relative atomic masses : Na = 23.0, Cl = 35.5)
children.answer:
Total mass of 4 Na⁺ ions and 4 Cl⁻ ions 
= (23.0 + 35.5) × 4 / L = 234 / L (g)

children.label: ii
children.question_text: Hence, calculate the Avogadro's constant L, given that 1.00 cm³ of sodium chloride crystal weighs 2.17 g.

questions.subject: Chemistry
topics: Topic 5: Fossil Fuels and Carbon Compounds
questions.question_text:
An experiment was carried out as shown below:

[IMG:q3_img_0.png]

children.label: i
children.question_text: Suggest what the orange organic solution may be.
children.answer: bromine in organic solvent

children.label: ii
children.question_text: With the help of a chemical equation, explain the colour change in the solution.
children.answer:
•  \( CH_{3}-CH=CH-CH_{3} + Br_{2} \rightarrow CH_{3}-(CHBr)_{2}-CH_{3} \)  
• But-2-ene / An alkene reacts with  \( Br_{2} \) , and  \( Br_{2} \)  is decolourised / all  \( Br_{2} \)  is consumed / a colourless product is formed.

sub_questions.label: b
sub_questions.question_text:
Another experiment was carried out as shown below :

[IMG:q3_img_1.png]

With the help of a chemical equation, suggest and explain what gas X may be.
sub_questions.answer:
• Gas X may be ammonia / NH₃.
• NH₃(aq) + H₂O(l) ⇌ NH₄⁺(aq) + OH⁻(aq)
• OH⁻(aq) turns phenolphthalein pink. / Ammonia / The gas / The solution is alkaline, and it turns phenolphthalein pink.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.question_text:
Solid potassium hydrogenphthalate can be used to prepare standard solutions. Its structure is shown below:

[IMG:q4_img_0.png]

sub_questions.label: a
sub_questions.question_text: You are provided with 1.12 g of solid potassium hydrogenphthalate.

children.label: i
children.question_text: Describe briefly how a 250.0 cm\( ^{3} \)  of standard solution containing 1.12 g of potassium hydrogenphthalate can be prepared in a laboratory.
children.answer:
• Dissolve the solid by adding deionised / distilled water to the solid in a beaker.
• Transfer the solution with rinsing with deionised / distilled water into a 250.0 cm³ volumetric flask and add deionised / distilled water to the graduation mark of the flask and shake thoroughly.

children.label: ii
children.question_text:
Calculate the molarity of the standard solution obtained in (i). 
(Formula mass : potassium hydrogenphthalate = 204.1)
children.answer:
molarity of the standard solution 
=  \( (1.12 / 204.1) \div 0.2500 \)
= 0.022 (M)

sub_questions.label: b
sub_questions.question_text: At room conditions, the pH of a 0.060 M of potassium hydrogenphthalate solution is 3.30. Based on this information and appropriate calculation, comment whether the -COOH group in potassium hydrogenphthalate is completely ionised.
sub_questions.answer:
• If it ionises completely in water,  \( [H^{+}(aq)] = 0.060 \, M \)  then the pH will be 1.22.
• However, the actual pH (3.30) is higher than 1.22, therefore the -COOH group in potassium hydrogenphthalate only ionises partly in water.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.question_text:
The structure of a compound is shown below:

[IMG:q5_img_0.png]

Reacting with a reagent under certain conditions, it can give two compounds with the same molecular formula  \( C_{5}H_{10}Cl_{2} \)  but different structures.

sub_questions.label: a
sub_questions.question_text: Suggest what the reagent is.
sub_questions.answer: chlorine / Cl₂

sub_questions.label: b
sub_questions.question_text: State the condition needed for the reaction to occur at room temperature.
sub_questions.answer: light / hν / ultra-violet / UV / radical initiator

sub_questions.label: c
sub_questions.question_text: Name the type of the reaction involved.
sub_questions.answer: substitution reaction

children.label: i
children.question_text: Draw the structure of ONE of these two compounds and give its systematic name.
children.answer:
[IMG:q5_img_1.png] | [IMG:q5_img_2.png]
1,3-dichloro-2,2-dimethylpropane | 1,1-dichloro-2,2-dimethylpropane

children.label: ii
children.question_text: Draw the structure of the other compound.
children.answer:
[IMG:q5_img_1.png] | [IMG:q5_img_2.png]
1,3-dichloro-2,2-dimethylpropane | 1,1-dichloro-2,2-dimethylpropane 

The structure other to the answer in (i)

children.label: iii
children.question_text: These two compounds are isomers. State the type of isomerism exhibited by them.
children.answer: structural isomer

sub_questions.label: a
sub_questions.question_text: Draw the three-dimensional structure of a  \( CH_{2}Cl_{2} \)  molecule.
sub_questions.answer: [IMG:q6_img_0.png]

children.label: i
children.question_text: Explain why  \( CH_{2}Cl_{2} \)  is a polar molecule but  \( CCl_{4} \)  is not.
children.answer: The polarities of bonds in  \( CCl_{4} \)  cancel out each other while those in  \( CH_{2}Cl_{2} \)  do not.

children.label: ii
children.question_text: Explain why  \( CCl_{4} \)  has a higher boiling point than  \( CH_{2}Cl_{2} \) .
children.answer:
•  \( CCl_{4} \)  has a larger molecular size than that of  \( CH_{2}Cl_{2} \) .
• Therefore, it has stronger van der Waals' forces between molecules / intermolecular forces, and hence a higher boiling point.

questions.subject: Chemistry
topics: Topic 7: Redox Reactions, Chemical Cells and Electrolysis

sub_questions.label: a
sub_questions.question_text:
Consider the chemical cell as shown below :

[IMG:q7_img_0.png]

children.label: i
children.question_text: What is the function of the porous pot?
children.answer: Separate the  \( CuSO_{4}(aq) \)  and  \( MgSO_{4}(aq) \) . / Allow ions to pass through. / Complete the circuit.

children.label: ii
children.question_text: Deduce whether the electrons flow through the external circuit from the magnesium electrode to the copper electrode.
children.answer: Yes. The multimeter reading is positive showing electrons flowing from Mg to Cu through the external circuit as Mg loses electrons more readily than Cu.

children.label: iii
children.question_text: Write the half equation for the change that occurs at the cathode.
children.answer: \( \mathrm{Cu^{2+}(aq) + 2e^{-} \rightarrow Cu(s)} \)

sub_questions.label: b
sub_questions.question_text:
Consider another chemical cell as shown below :

[IMG:q7_img_1.png]

children.label: i
children.question_text: Write the half equation for the change that occurs at the graphite electrode.
children.answer: \( Br_{2}(aq) + 2e^{-} \rightarrow 2Br^{-}(aq) \)

children.label: ii
children.question_text: State the expected observation at the copper electrode.
children.answer: The size of the electrode decreases. / Colour around the electrode deepens.

children.label: iii
children.question_text: Would the multimeter reading become more negative, less negative or remain unchanged if the solution of  \( Br_{2}(aq) \)  and  \( KBr(aq) \)  is replaced by a solution of  \( I_{2}(aq) \)  and  \( KI(aq) \) , while the other conditions remain unchanged? Explain your answer.
children.answer:
• less negative
• Iodine gains electrons less readily than bromine.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.question_text:
Several trials of an experiment were performed for determining the enthalpy change of neutralisation for a reaction. For each trial, a total volume of \( 100.0 \, cm^{3} \) of a solution was obtained from mixing specified volumes of a HCl(aq) and \( 1.0 \, M \, NaOH(aq) \) as shown below in an expanded polystyrene cup. The HCl(aq) and NaOH(aq) were kept at the same initial temperature before mixing.
Trial12345
Volume of the HCl(aq) used / cm³9070503010
Volume of 1.0 M NaOH(aq) used / cm³1030507090
For each trial, the mixture was stirred and its maximum temperature reached was recorded. A graph of the maximum temperature reached for each trial is shown below: [IMG:q8_img_0.png]
questions.answer:
(a)
Maximum temp = 33.1°C
Straight Line
Straight Line
58.0 cm³
25.5°C

sub_questions.label: a
sub_questions.question_text: It is estimated from the graph that 58.0 cm³ of NaOH(aq) (and 42.0 cm³ of HCl(aq)) is required for obtaining the possible maximum temperature reached in this experiment. Show how this estimation can be done in the above graph.
sub_questions.answer: [IMG:q8_img_1.png]

children.label: i
children.question_text: Calculate the number of moles of NaOH(aq) reacted with HCl(aq) in (a). Hence, find the concentration of the HCl(aq).
children.answer:
No. of moles of NaOH(aq) used = 1.0 × (58.0 ÷ 1000) = 0.058
At equivalent point, no. of moles of NaOH(aq) used = No. of moles of HCl(aq) reacted
∴ No. of moles of HCl(aq) reacted = 0.058
Concentration of HCl(aq) = 0.058 ÷ (42.0 ÷ 1000) = 1.38 mol dm⁻³

children.label: ii
children.answer:
Energy released during the reaction = 100.0 × 1.0 × 4.18 × (33.1 − 25.5) = 3176.8 J
Enthalpy change of neutralisation = −3176.8 ÷ (0.058 × 1000) = −54.77 (kJ mol⁻¹)

sub_questions.label: c
sub_questions.question_text: The one determined above is not the standard enthalpy change of neutralisation. What, then, is meant by the term ‘standard enthalpy change of neutralisation’?
sub_questions.answer: The term means the enthalpy change when an acid solution and a base / an alkali solution react together under standard conditions to produce 1 mole of water.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.question_text: Iron cans used to store food products are commonly coated with a thin layer of tin.

sub_questions.label: a
sub_questions.question_text: The thin layer of tin prevents iron cans from corrosion.

children.label: i
children.question_text: Briefly describe the principle for this kind of corrosion prevention.
children.answer: Prevents the iron from contacting with air / oxygen / water.

children.label: ii
children.question_text: Explain whether these iron cans would corrode more readily once their surfaces are damaged by scratching.
children.answer: Yes. These iron cans corrode more readily as tin is less reactive than iron.

children.label: iii
children.question_text: Suggest why galvanisation is not suitable to prevent corrosion in iron cans that are used to store food products.
children.answer: Zinc ions are toxic and may contaminate the food.

sub_questions.label: b
sub_questions.question_text: There is an increasing trend for manufacturers to use cans made entirely of aluminium for storing food products.

children.label: i
children.question_text: Explain why aluminium is more resistant to corrosion than iron, although it occupies a higher position than iron in the reactivity series.
children.answer: Aluminium has an impervious / impermeable layer of oxide / aluminium oxide on the surface.

children.label: ii
children.question_text: Name the process that increases the corrosion resistance of aluminium cans.
children.answer: anodisation

children.label: iii
children.question_text: Other than corrosion resistance, suggest one advantage of using aluminium to make cans.
children.answer: It has a low density / is malleable / is easy to mould / recycle / dye.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.question_text:
You are provided with common laboratory apparatus and the following chemicals :
\(iron\ powder \quad zinc\ powder \quad aqueous\ ammonia \quad distilled\ water\)
Describe how zinc sulphate crystals can be obtained from a solid sample of zinc sulphate containing copper(II) sulphate as impurity. (Not all chemicals must be used.)
questions.answer:
Chemical knowledge 
• Dissolve the sample in distilled water.
• Add excess  \( Zn(s) \)  to the sample solution.
• Filter to collect  \( ZnSO_{4}(aq) \) .
• Evaporate the filtrate, allow  \( ZnSO_{4} \)  solid to crystallise out / collect crystals and then dry with filter paper / in a desiccator.
Communication mark

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.question_text:
Two trials of an experiment were performed using the set-up below to study the reaction between nitric acid and calcium carbonate. A gas was formed in the reaction. [IMG:q11_img_0.png] The chemicals used are listed in the table below while other experimental conditions were the same.
TrialMass of \(CaCO_3(s)\) added / gVolume of 3.0 M \(HNO_3(aq)\) added / \(cm^3\)Volume of \(H_2O(l)\) added / \(cm^3\)
13.010.020.0
23.020.010.0

sub_questions.label: a
sub_questions.question_text: Write the chemical equation for the reaction between nitric acid and calcium carbonate.
sub_questions.answer:
\( \mathrm{CaCO_{3}(s)+2H^{+}(aq)\rightarrow Ca^{2+}(aq)+CO_{2}(g)+H_{2}O(l)} \)  /
\( \mathrm{CaCO_{3}(s)+2HNO_{3}(aq)\rightarrow Ca(NO_{3})_{2}(aq)+CO_{2}(g)+H_{2}O(l)} \)

sub_questions.label: b
sub_questions.question_text:
The graph below shows the variation of the mass measured with time for the two trials.

[IMG:q11_img_1.png]

children.label: i
children.question_text: Calculate the average rate of formation of the gas from the \(2^{nd}\) minute to the \(12^{th}\) minute for Trial 2.

sub_questions.label: c
sub_questions.question_text: Suggest how the effect of surface area of solid reactant on the rate of reaction can be studied using the above set-up.
sub_questions.answer: Use same mass of calcium carbonate of different sizes to perform the experiment, all other conditions of the experiment should be kept unchanged.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.question_text:
Consider an equilibrium mixture of the following chemical reaction :
\( \begin{array}{c}\mathrm{Fe}^{3+}(\mathrm{aq})+\mathrm{SCN}^{-}(\mathrm{aq})\rightleftharpoons\mathrm{Fe}(\mathrm{SCN})^{2+}(\mathrm{aq})\\\mathrm{yellow}\quad\mathrm{colourless}\quad\mathrm{deep\ red}\end{array} \)

sub_questions.label: a
sub_questions.question_text: Write an expression for the equilibrium constant  \( K_{c} \)  for the reaction.
sub_questions.answer: \( [\mathrm{Fe}(\mathrm{SCN})^{2+}(\mathrm{aq})] / [\mathrm{Fe}^{3+}(\mathrm{aq})][\mathrm{SCN}^{-}(\mathrm{aq})] \)

sub_questions.label: b
sub_questions.question_text: At a certain temperature, the equilibrium constant  \( K_{c} \)  for the reaction is  \( 1.08 \times 10^{3} \, dm^{3} \, mol^{-1} \) . The equilibrium mixture is prepared by mixing  \( 20.0 \, cm^{3} \)  of  \( 0.030 \, M \, Fe(NO_{3})_{3}(aq) \)  with  \( 10.0 \, cm^{3} \)  of  \( 0.030 \, M \, KSCN(aq) \)  in an acidic medium. Calculate the concentration of  \( \mathrm{Fe(SCN)^{2+}(aq)} \)  in the equilibrium mixture at that temperature.
sub_questions.answer:
\( \mathrm{Fe}^{3+}(\mathrm{aq}) + \mathrm{SCN}^{-}(\mathrm{aq}) \rightleftharpoons \mathrm{Fe}(\mathrm{SCN})^{2+}(\mathrm{aq}) \)
0.020 - x        0.010 - x
x / (0.020 - x)(0.010 - x) = 1.08 × 10³
x = 0.0217 mol dm⁻³ (rejected since larger than both 0.020 and 0.010)
x = 9.21 × 10⁻³ mol dm⁻³

sub_questions.label: c
sub_questions.question_text: It is known that the equilibrium constant  \( K_{c} \)  increases when temperature increases. Suggest and explain whether the enthalpy change of the reaction is positive, negative or zero.
sub_questions.answer: Increasing of  \( K_{e} \)  means that the equilibrium position shifts to the right / product side, hence the  \( \Delta H \)  should be positive.

sub_questions.label: d
sub_questions.question_text: When a little amount of  \( Na_{2}SO_{3}(s) \)  is added to the equilibrium mixture, the colour of the mixture becomes paler. Explain this observation.
sub_questions.answer:
•  \( Na_{2}SO_{3} \) (s) added reacts with  \( Fe^{3+} \) (aq) so as to decrease the concentration of  \( Fe^{3+} \) (aq).
• The equilibrium position shifts to the left / reactant side. The concentration of  \( \mathrm{Fe}(\mathrm{SCN})^{2+} \)  decreases, so the colour of the mixture becomes paler.

sub_questions.label: a
sub_questions.question_text:
It was intended to prepare ethanoic acid from ethanol by the following set-up. However, the distillate collected mainly contained another organic product X but not ethanoic acid.

[IMG:q13_img_0.png]

children.label: i
children.question_text: What is X?
children.answer: ethanal / acetaldehyde / CH₃CHO

children.label: ii
children.question_text: Explain why the distillate collected mainly contained X but not ethanoic acid.
children.answer: It is because ethanal has a low boiling point / is volatile, so was easily distilled off / vaporised out and cannot be further oxidised to give ethanoic acid.

sub_questions.label: b
sub_questions.question_text: Ethanoic acid can be converted to an unsubstituted amide.

children.label: i
children.question_text: Give the systematic name of this amide.
children.answer: ethanamide

children.label: ii
children.question_text: Suggest what reagent and condition are needed for this conversion.

sub_questions.label: c
sub_questions.question_text:
The following shows the formation of a polymer from an amide :

[IMG:q13_img_1.png]
sub_questions.answer:
(i)
1
H
N
1

children.label: i
children.question_text: Draw the repeating unit of the polymer formed.
children.answer: [IMG:q13_img_2.png]

children.label: ii
children.question_text: There is a view which suggests that the above polymerisation does not involve condensation. Give a reason to support this view.
children.answer: As there is no losing of small molecules during the polymerisation, it can be regarded as no condensation is involved.

questions.subject: Chemistry
topics: Topic 12: Patterns in the Chemical World
questions.question_text:
The following graph shows an incomplete sketch of the variation in melting points of the elements in the third period of the Periodic Table.

[IMG:q14_img_0.png]

sub_questions.label: a
sub_questions.question_text: Complete the sketch on the graph above.
sub_questions.answer: [IMG:ans_q14_a_0.jpg]

sub_questions.label: b
sub_questions.question_text: Explain why the melting point of Mg is higher than that of Na.
sub_questions.answer:
The metallic bond in Mg is stronger than that in Na as Mg has more delocalised electrons / more outermost shell electrons than Na. OR
The metallic bond in Mg is stronger than that in Na as Mg has two outermost shell delocalised electrons while Na has one only.

sub_questions.label: c
sub_questions.question_text: Explain why the melting point of Si is higher than that of P.
sub_questions.answer:
• Melting of Si needs high energy to break the strong covalent bonds between Si atoms in the giant covalent structure.
• Melting of P only needs smaller energy to break the weak intermolecular forces. / P has a simple molecular structure, there are weak van der Waals' forces between molecules.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.question_text:
With reference to the structure of sodium lauryl sulphate (SLS) below, explain why it has cleansing properties.

[IMG:q15_img_0.png]
questions.answer:
Chemical knowledge (1 mark for each point, a maximum of 4 marks) 
• It reduces the water surface tension so that water can spread and wet the surfaces.
• The hydrocarbon tails of the detergent particles dissolve in oil (hydrophobic).
• The ionic heads of the detergent particles dissolve in water (hydrophilic).
• Water molecules attract the hydrophilic ionic heads and bring the oil into water.
• By stirring, the oil breaks up into tiny droplets and these droplets cannot come together again due to the repulsion between ionic heads / negative charges.
Communication mark
